title: Loukoumades
subtitle: Greek honey fritters with cinnamon and walnuts
excerpt: Greek fried honey balls: a loose yeasted batter dropped in small spoonfuls into hot oil and fried until puffed and golden. Served immediately with a drizzle of warm honey, a scatter of crushed walnuts, and cinnamon. Greece's oldest street food.
sourceNotes: Loukoumades are among the oldest documented Greek foods, with fried honey-drenched pastries mentioned in classical Greek texts and attributed to the ancient Olympic victory celebrations. The modern yeasted loukoumas is documented in Greek culinary texts from the Byzantine period and appears in the fourteenth-century cookbook manuscript Hedypatheia by Diphilos of Siphnus. They are reproduced across public-domain Greek regional cookbooks and remain a ubiquitous street food throughout Greece and Cyprus.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Loukoumades must be served immediately from the oil. The batter can rest for up to 2 hours at room temperature. Do not refrigerate the batter.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: Deep-fried at 180°C. The batter is loose enough to self-round into balls as it hits the oil. Turning them continuously produces even colour on all surfaces.

glossaryTerms.definition: Unlike doughnut dough, loukoumas batter is too wet to shape by hand. It is dropped into hot oil by the spoonful using two spoons (or a small ice cream scoop) and rounds itself as it fries. The exterior crisps while the steam from the wet batter inflates the interior. The result is hollow at the centre, not solid.

content.text: Loukoumades differ from doughnuts because the batter is a loose, pourable consistency, not a dough.

produces the hollow centre characteristic of loukoumades. They must be eaten immediately — within minutes of coming out of the oil. Honey goes on hot, the walnuts and cinnamon go on the honey, and they are eaten at once.

Whisk the flour, yeast, sugar, and salt together. Add the warm water and whisk to a smooth, thick batter — the consistency of thick double cream. Cover and rest at room temperature for 1 hour until the batter is bubbly and has risen visibly.

Heat the oil in a large saucepan to 180°C. Using two wet dessertspoons, scoop a heaped spoonful of batter and use the second spoon to push it off into the oil. Each should be roughly the size of a ping-pong ball. Fry in batches of 6–8, turning continuously with a slotted spoon, for 3–4 minutes until deep amber all over. They will self-round as they cook. Drain briefly.
Pile the hot loukoumades into a bowl. Drizzle the warm honey over liberally, scatter the crushed walnuts, and dust with cinnamon. Serve within 5 minutes — they lose their crunch quickly.
